game_name: Fire Emblem: Shadow Dragon and the Blade of Light

design_philosophy.core_loop: Deploy units on grid-based maps, move strategically, engage in turn-based combat, manage limited resources (weapons degrade, permanent death), progress through linear story chapters
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Permanent death mechanic creates irreversible loss; resource scarcity (limited currency, weapon durability) forces tactical spending decisions; unit positioning and class matchups determine combat outcomes
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command a growing army as a noble prince reclaiming his kingdom; recruit diverse character classes with unique abilities; experience emergent narratives through permadeath consequences
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Codifying design: permanent permadeath as core mechanic (no resurrection), class-based unit identity system with weapon limitations, grid-based tactical positioning as primary decision space. Rejects convenience in favor of consequence and strategic planning

difficulty.lives_system: Permadeath per unit; game over only if Marth falls
difficulty.checkpointing: Chapter-based progression; no mid-chapter saves implied
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Unit recruitment choices, deployment positioning, weapon/item management, and arena wagering for currency create meaningful difficulty modulation through player decisions

progression.upgrade_system: Level-up system: defeated enemies yield experience; 100 EXP triggers level-up with random stat increases per class. Weapon acquisition via towns and secret vendors. Class-specific stat growth rates
progression.unlock_mechanism: Linear chapter unlock; story gates map progression. 52 recruitable characters unlock through story events and recruitment conversations. Character promotions available through promotion items
replayability_hooks: Permadeath creates unique outcomes per playthrough based on unit losses
52 recruitable characters with varied class types allow different army compositions
Arena battles available for currency wagering
Multiple unused characters in data (Sabre, others) suggest content removal or alternative designs

world_building_techniques: Character recruitment conversations tied to narrative events
Village and house dialogue revealing world state and NPC perspectives
Character endings epilogue entries showing post-war fates
Environmental storytelling through map progression across Archanea continent

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters as narrative culminations (commanders, final antagonists Gharnef and Medeus); boss units distinguished from generic enemies through story significance and placement as map objectives
notable_bosses: Michalis (appears to have significant story/data presence based on portrait reuse)

faction_indicators: Kingdoms of Macedon and Grust (allied with Dolhr)
danger_signals: Resource scarcity forcing difficult economic decisions
Permadeath of protagonist Marth triggers game over
environmental_storytelling: Map progression across Archanea continent shows territorial control shifts
Town and village locations accessible during campaigns reveal NPC perspectives
Kingdom liberation sequences mark story progression
Secret vendors indicate underground resistance networks
Sacred locations (Falchion theft, Fire Emblem acquisition) anchor narrative stakes
